modules.name: py3.appy.database.sorter
documentation: Sort results produced by a catalog

classes.name: py3.appy.database.sorter.Sorter

methods.name: py3.appy.database.sorter.Sorter.__init__
documentation: The sorter' input and output data structures are as follows.
input   | A result set of object IIDs as computed by a catalog, as an
| instance of IISet.
output  | A sorted list of object IIDs, in the form of a Lazy data
| structure (see appy/database/lazy.py). The sorter produces an
| intermediary data structure that is more complex than a list.
| Instead of directly and completely "flattening" this data
| structure into a list of IIDs, we wrap it, for performance
| reasons, into a Lazy data structure, that behaves like a list
| and manipulates, behind the scenes, the more complex internal
| structure.

methods.name: py3.appy.database.sorter.Sorter.iterateByIndex
documentation:
Fills list p_r with sorted entries from p_self.rs, by iterating over
p_self.sortIndex.

methods.name: py3.appy.database.sorter.Sorter.iterateByResultSet
documentation:
Fills list p_r with sorted entries frol p_self.rs by iterating over
p_self.rs.

methods.name: py3.appy.database.sorter.Sorter.run
documentation:
Returns a Lazy data structure containing object IIDs found in
p_self.rs, sorted by p_self.sortIndex.
Sorts results as produced by a catalog

methods.name: py3.appy.test.performance.Hit.sendOne
documentation:
Called by m_send below to send a single request to the server. If an
p_id(entifier) is given, it will be used to store the list of URLs
found in the response body in tester.responseUrls. Returns a tuple
(success, response).

methods.name: py3.appy.test.performance.Hit.send
documentation:
Send the request(s) corresponding to this hit to the tested server
and returns a tuple (success, response) containing the result of
executing the last request.

Represents a HTTP request on the server.
If your scenario simply consists in a series of HTTP GET requests on the
server, you do not need to use a Hit instance: simply define URLs as
strings within Config.scenario. When things get more complex, use a Hit
instance:
 * if you need to define a POST request;
 * if you need to define a URL by parsing the last (or a previous)
   request and finding your URL among it.

documentation: Copyright (C) 2007-2024 Gaetan Delannay
This file is part of Appy.
Appy is free software: you can redistribute it and/or modify it under the

Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or (at your option) any later

This module implements "integration" testing. It works by:
- defining an executable test plan as an ODT file containing tests as tables
conforming to conventions as described in appy/utils/tables.py ;
- executing this plan, or only a sub-part of its tests ;
- producing a report about success or failure of every test ran.

modules.name: py3.appy.database.indexes
documentation: Module defining database indexes

classes.name: py3.appy.database.indexes.Index

methods.name: py3.appy.database.indexes.Index.__init__
documentation: Index constructor
An index is made of 2 main dicts: "byValue" stores objects keyed by their
index values (this is the "forward" index), while "byObject" stores index
values keyed by object ID (this the "reverse" index).
Let's take an example. Suppose we want to index values for some
attribute on 3 objects. Here are the attribute values.
Object   | Attribute value
obj1     | value1
obj2     | value2
obj3     | value1
Here are the content for dicts "byValue" and "byObject".
Dict     | Value
byValue  | { value1: [obj1, obj3], value2: [obj2] }
byObject | { obj1: value1, obj2: value2, obj3: value1 }
In m_init below, you will get more precisions about the data structures in
use. For example, object integer IDs are stored (from their attribute
o.iid), and not the objects themselves. For more info about object IIDs,
see appy/database/__init__.py).

methods.name: py3.appy.database.lock.Lock.hasOn
documentation: Is a lock currently set on p_o ?
Locks on a given database object are implemented as a dict object.locks of
the form
~{s_page: (s_userLogin, DateTime_lockDate)}~

methods.name: py3.appy.database.lock.Lock.isSet
documentation: Is this p_page locked on p_o ?

methods.name: py3.appy.database.lock.Lock.set
documentation:
The current user edits a given p_page on p_o: set a lock, to prevent
other users to edit this page at the same time.

methods.name: py3.appy.database.lock.Lock.remove
documentation:
Removes the lock on p_o's p_page. This happens:
- after the page has been saved: the lock must be released;
- when an admin wants to force the deletion of a lock that was left
  on p_page for too long (p_force=True).

methods.name: py3.appy.database.lock.Lock.removeMine
documentation:
If p_user has set a lock on p_page, this method removes it. This
method is called when the user that locked a page consults PX "view"
for this page. In this case, we consider that the user has left the
edit page in an unexpected way and we remove the lock.

methods.name: py3.appy.database.lock.Lock.getLockers
documentation:
Returns the logins (or names if p_names is True) of the users having
locked at least on page on p_o. The p_result is a list, excepted
if p_sep is given: in that case, it is a string, joined via p_sep.

methods.name: py3.appy.database.lock.Lock.unlockableBy
documentation: May the currently logged p_user unlock p_o ?

methods.name: py3.appy.database.lock.Lock.unlock
documentation:
Called when the logged user wants to remove a lock that was left for
too long by some user.

A lock is a database structure remembering which user (login) has locked
which page on which object.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.test.Tester.AnnotationsRemover
documentation:
This parser is used to remove from content.xml and styles.xml the
Python tracebacks that may be dumped into OpenDocument annotations by
pod when generating errors. Indeed, those tracebacks contain lot of
machine-specific info, like absolute paths to the python files, etc.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.pod_parser.OdInsert
documentation:
While parsing an odt/pod file, we may need to insert a specific odt chunk
at a given place in the odt file (ie: add the pod-specific fonts and
styles). OdInsert instances define such 'inserts' (what to insert and
when).

Now the namespaces were parsed; p_inserts can be set in the form of a

We just dumped the start of an elem. Here we will insert any odt

Propagates the namespaces in all XML element definitions that are

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.DocImporter

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.DocImporter.checkAt
documentation: Check and apply some transform to p_at

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.DocImporter.getImportFolder
documentation:
This method gives the path where to dump the content of the document
or image. In the case of a document it is a temp folder; in the case
of an image it is a folder within the ODT result.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.DocImporter.getImportPath
documentation:
Gets the path name of the file to dump on disk (within the ODT for
images, in a temp folder for docs).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.DocImporter.moveFile
documentation:
In the case "self.at" was used, we may want to move the file at
self.at within the ODT result in self.importPath (for images) or do
nothing (for docs). In the latter case, the file to import stays
at self.at, and is not copied into self.importPath. So the previously
computed self.importPath is not used at all.

Base class used for importing external content into a pod template (an
image, another pod template, another odt document...

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.OdtImporter

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.OdtImporter.init
documentation: OdtImporter-specific constructor

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.OdtImporter.getPageBreakAfterType
documentation:
After sub-document insertion, must we insert a page break of type
"after" or "before"?

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.OdtImporter.run
documentation: Import the content of a sub-ODT document

This class allows to import the content of another ODT document into a
pod template.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.PodImporter

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.PodImporter.mustStream
documentation:
Returns "in" if communication with LibreOffice must be performed via
streams.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.PodImporter.init
documentation: PodImporter-specific constructor

Imports the result of applying another POD template, into the current POD
result.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.PdfImporter
documentation:
This class allows to import the content of a PDF file into a pod
template. It calls gs to split the PDF into images and calls the
ImageImporter for importing it into the result.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ConvertImporter
documentation:
This class allows to import the content of any file that LibreOffice (LO)
can convert into PDF: doc(x), rtf, xls(x)... It first calls LO to convert
the document into PDF, then calls a PdfImporter.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.Image

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.Image.getSizeIn
documentation: Reads the first bytes from the image on disk to get its size

Represents an image on disk. This class is used to detect the image type
and size.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ImageImporter

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ImageImporter.isImage
documentation:
Cheks that the p_response received after a HTTP GET supposed to
retrieve an image actually contains an image.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ImageImporter.checkAt
documentation:
Do not raise an exception when p_at does not correspond to an
existing file. We will dump a replacement image instead.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ImageImporter.moveFile
documentation: Copies file at self.at into the ODT file at self.importPath

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ImageImporter.manageExif
documentation:
Read, with ImageMagick, EXIF metadata in the image, and apply a
rotation (still with ImageMagick) if required.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ImageImporter.init
documentation: ImageImporter-specific constructor

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ImageImporter.applyMax
documentation:
Return a tuple (width, height), potentially modified w.r.t. p_width
and p_height, to take care of p_self.maxWidth & p_self.maxHeight.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.doc_importers.ImageImporter.getImageSize
documentation:
Get or compute the image size and returns the corresponding ODF
attributes specifying image width and height expressed in cm.

This class allows to import into the ODT result an image stored
externally.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.setFrom
documentation: Associate to this action a "from" clause (pod only)

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.getExceptionLine
documentation:
Gets the line describing exception p_e, containing the exception
class, message and line number.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.manageError
documentation:
Manage the encountered error: dump it into the buffer or raise an
exception.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action._evalExpr
documentation:
Evaluates p_expr with p_context. p_expr can contain an error expr,
in the form "normalExpr|errorExpr". If it is the case, if the
"normal" expression raises an error, the "error" expression is
evaluated instead.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.storeExprResult
documentation: Store this expression evaluation p_r(esult) in the context

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.evaluateExpression
documentation:
Evaluates expression p_expr with the current p_context. Returns a
tuple (result, errorOccurred).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.execute
documentation:
Executes this action given some p_context and add the result to
p_result.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.getBufferSlice
documentation:
If only a slice of the tied buffer must be evaluated, this method
returns a tuple (startIndex, endIndex). In that case, only
p_self.buffer.content[startIndex, endIndex] will be dumped in the POD
result.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.evaluateBuffer
documentation:
Evaluates the buffer tied to this action and add the result in
p_result. The source for evaluation can be forced to p_forceSource
but in most cases depends on self.source.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.addSubAction
documentation: Adds p_action as a sub-action of this action

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.getAction
documentation:
If p_self or one of its sub-actions (recursively) is of this p_type,
returns it.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Action.check
documentation:
Returns a tuple (success, message) indicating if the action is well
formed or not.

Abstract class representing a action (=statement) that must be performed
on the content of a buffer (if, for...).

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.If
documentation:
Action that determines if we must include the content of the buffer in
the result or not.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Else

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Else.do
documentation:
Execute this "else" action: it occurs when the tied "if" action is
not executed.

Action that is linked to a previous "if" action. In fact, an "else"
action works exactly like an "if" action, excepted that instead of
defining a conditional expression, it is based on the negation of the
conditional expression of the last defined "if" action.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.For

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.For.initialiseLoop
documentation:
Initialises information about the loop, before entering into it. It
is possible that this loop overrides an outer loop whose iterator
has the same name. This method returns a tuple
(loop, outerOverriddenLoop).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.For.updateContext
documentation:
We are in the loop, and p_item is the currently walked item. We must
update the context by adding or updating values for iterator
variable(s).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.For.do
documentation:
Performs the "for" action. p_elems is the list of elements to
walk, evaluated from self.expr.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.For.getBufferSlice
documentation:
When processing a "do doc" statement, depending on the current
iteration, only a specific slice of the buffer must be dumped.

Actions that will include the content of the buffer as many times as
specified by the action parameters.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Null

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Null.check
documentation: This action must have a tied from clause

Action that does nothing. Used in conjunction with a "from" clause, it
allows to insert in a buffer arbitrary odt content.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.MetaIf

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.MetaIf.reifyNote
documentation: Recreate the note as close to the original as possible

Action allowing a note not to be evaluated and re-dumped as-is in the
result, depending on some (meta-) condition.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Variables

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Variables.storeVariable
documentation:
Adds a variable named p_name with this p_value in the p_context.
Updates the dict of p_hidden variables and r_eturn it.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Variables.removeVariable
documentation: Remove, when relevant, variable p_name from the p_context

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.actions.Variables.do
documentation: Evaluate the variables' expressions

Action that allows to define a set of variables somewhere in the
template.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.parts.OdtTable

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.parts.OdtTable.dumpCell
documentation:
Dumps a cell in the table. If no specific p_paraStyle (p_cellStyle)
is given, self.paraStyle (self.cellStyle) is used, excepted if
p_header is True: in that case, self.paraHeaderStyle
(self.cellHeaderStyle) is used. p_align is used only for HTML.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.parts.OdtTable.get
documentation: Returns the whole table.

This class allows to construct an ODT table programmatically. As ODT and
HTML are very similar, this class also allows to contruct an
HTML table.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.Buffer

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.Buffer.crunchExpr
documentation: Removes any unnecessary whitespace in this parsed p_expr(ession)

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.Buffer.patchProtected
documentation:
Add or patch the attribute allowing to protect this p_elem (a cell or
section), by evaluating variable "cellProtected" or
"sectionProtected".

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.Buffer.patchElement
documentation:
Modifies p_elem's attributes when relevant and make them dependent of
some variable definitions.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.Buffer.dumpStartElement
documentation:
Inserts into this buffer the start tag p_elem, with its p_attrs,
excepted those listed in p_ignoreAttrs.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.Buffer.dumpEndElement
documentation: Dump this end p_elem into p_self

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.Buffer.dumpElement
documentation: For dumping a whole element at once

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.Buffer.dumpContent
documentation: Dumps string p_content into the buffer
Abstract class representing any buffer used during rendering

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.FileBuffer.getLength
documentation: getLength is used to manage insertions into sub-buffers. But in the case
of a FileBuffer, we will only have 1 sub-buffer at a time, and we don't
care about where it will be inserted into the FileBuffer.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.clone
documentation: Produces an empty buffer that is a clone of this one

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.getInsertIndex
documentation:
Return, within this buffer or any of its sub-buffers, the index that
immediately follows the first occurrence of p_tag, which is an
opening or ending tag, depending on p_opening.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.insertSubBuffer
documentation:
Inserts p_subBuffer just after the main opening tag, in p_self that
is already complete.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.getRootBuffer
documentation:
Returns the root buffer. For POD it is always a FileBuffer. For PX,
it is a MemoryBuffer.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.write
documentation: Adds p_thing to p_self.content

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.isMainElement
documentation: Is p_elem the main element within this buffer?

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.pushSubBuffer
documentation: Sets p_subBuffer at the very end of the buffer

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.transferAllContent
documentation: Transfer all content to parent

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.addExpression
documentation: Creates an Expression object and add it in the buffer

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.addAttributes
documentation: pod-only: adds an Attributes object into this buffer

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.addAttribute
documentation: px-only: adds an Attribute object into this buffer

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer._getVariables
documentation:
Returns variable definitions in p_expr as a list
~[(s_varName|[s_varName], s_expr)]~.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer._getForIterators
documentation:
Gets the variables used as iterators in a "for" statement, from
comma-separated string p_iters.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.getLinkableIf
documentation:
An (unnamed) "else" action needs to be created on this p_podElem.
Find and return an appropriate "if" from p_self.env.ifActions.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.createAction
documentation: Creates an Action object, depending on p_actionType

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.createActions
documentation:
Tries to create action(s) based on p_statements. If the statement is
not correct, r_ is -1. Else, r_ is the index of the element within
the buffer that is the object of the action(s).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.createPxAction
documentation:
Creates a PX action and link it to this buffer. If an action is
already linked to this buffer (in self.action), this action is
chained behind the last action via self.action.subAction.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.cut
documentation:
Cuts this buffer into 2 parts. Depending on p_keepFirstPart, the 1st
(from 0 to index-1) or the second (from index to the end) part of the
buffer is returned as a MemoryBuffer instance without parent; the
other part is self.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.getStartIndex
documentation:
When I must dump the buffer, sometimes (if p_removeMainElems is
True), I must dump only a subset of it. This method returns the start
index of the buffer part I must dump.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.getStopIndex
documentation: Return the stop index of the buffer part that must be dumped

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.removeAutomaticExpressions
documentation:
When a buffer has an action with minus=True, we must remove the
expressions automatically inserted by pod ("columnsRepeat"
"cellProtected"...) . Else, we will have problems when computing the
index of the part to keep (m_getStartIndex).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.evaluate
documentation:
Evaluates this buffer given the current p_context and add the result
into p_result. With pod, p_result is the root file buffer; with px
it is a memory buffer.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.clean
documentation: Cleans the buffer content

inner_classes.name: py3.appy.pod.buffers.MemoryBuffer.Rex
documentation: Regular expressions in use for a memory buffer
Buffer whose content is loaded in RAM

p_result can be the aobsolute path to the result to produce or only a
file extension indicating the format of the file to produce. This
method returns this format.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getCustomFunctions
documentation:
Compiles and executes the Python p_script and returns a dict
containing its namespace.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getFilePath
documentation:
Returns the absolute path of p_filePath. In fact, it returns a
tuple with some URL version of the path for LO as the first element
and the absolute path as the second element.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getResultFilter
documentation:
Based on the result type, identifies which OO filter to use for the
document conversion.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getResultUrl
documentation:
Returns the path of the result file in the format needed by LO. If
p_result is a file path, it will be used as base. Else, it is a file
extension (=the "result type"): computing the complete path will be
done as follows.

If the result type and the input type are the same (ie the user wants
to refresh indexes or some other action and not perform a real
conversion), the result file will be named
                <inputFileName>.res.<resultType>

Else, the result file will be named like the input file but with a
different extension:
                <inputFileName>.<resultType>

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.props
documentation:
Create a UNO-compliant tuple of properties, from tuple p_properties
containing sub-tuples (s_propertyName, value).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getConfig
documentation:
Returns a node within the LO configuration, stored at p_path. If the
objective is to update it, set p_update to True.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getVersion
documentation: Returns the LO version

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.connect
documentation: Connects to LibreOffice

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getColumnModifiers
documentation:
Returns the elements allowing to know if we must optimize or
distribute table columns.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.converter.Converter.processTableColumns
documentation:
Process column widths for this p_table: either p_optimize them or
p_distribute them evenly (one of these 2 params must be True). Note
that:
* the "optimize" algorithm dos not work properly in all cases, unless
  algorithm "distribute" is executed beforehand. So, in short:
           optimize = distribute + optimize
* if both p_optimize and p_distribute are specified, the p_optimize
  operation will be performed and will produce the final result.
  Indeed, even if a "distribute" preamble operation is performed, it
  will not be the final result.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.HtmlTable

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.HtmlTable.hasBorder
documentation:
Support for table borders is currently limited. We have 2 cases:
either we set a border to the table, either we do not. This method
r_eturns a boolean value reflecting this, based on the CSS or HTML
attribute "border". By default, we set a border.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.HtmlTable.setTableStyle
documentation:
The default ODT style "podTable" will apply to the table, excepted if
specific style elements are specified. In this case, we will create a
dynamic style whose parent will be "podTable". This method returns a
tuple (styleName, tableWidthPx). The table width in pixels is
sometimes needed to convert column widths, expressed in pixels, to
percentages.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.HtmlTable.setColumnWidth
documentation:
A p_width is defined for the current cell. Store it in
self.columnWidths

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.HtmlTable.softenWidths
documentation: Reduce columns widths being proportionnaly too large

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.HtmlTable.computeColumnStyles
documentation:
Once the table has been completely parsed, self.columnContentSizes
should be correctly filled. Based on this, we can deduce the width
of every column and create the corresponding style declarations, in
p_self.env.stylesManager.dynamicStyles.

Represents an HTML table, and also a sub-buffer. When parsing elements
corresponding to an HTML table (<table>, <tr>, <td>, etc), we can't dump
corresponding ODF elements directly into the global result buffer
(XhtmlEnvironment.res). Indeed, when dumping an ODF table, we must
dump columns declarations at the beginning of the table. So before
dumping rows and cells, we must know how much columns will be present
in the table. It means that we must first parse the first <tr> entirely
in order to know how much columns are present in the HTML table before
dumping the ODF table. So we use this class as a sub-buffer that will
be constructed as we parse the HTML table; when encountering the end
of the HTML table, we will dump the result of this sub-buffer into
the parent buffer, which may be the global buffer or another table
buffer.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.XhtmlEnvironment.getListStyle

Gets the list style to apply to p_xhtmlElem (a "ol" or "ul"). If no

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.XhtmlEnvironment.getTags

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.XhtmlEnvironment.closeConflictualElements

tags would be empty. In this latter case, tags are purely removed

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.XhtmlEnvironment.dumpString
documentation:
Dumps arbitrary content p_s.
If the table stack is not empty, we must dump p_s into the buffer
corresponding to the last parsed table. Else, we must dump p_s
into the global buffer (self.res).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.XhtmlEnvironment.getTagsToReopen
documentation:
Normally, tags to reopen are equal to p_conflictElems. But we have a
special case. Indeed, if a conflict elem has itself tagsToClose,
the last tag to close may not be needed anymore on the tag to
reopen, so we remove it.

p_elem.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.xhtml2odt.XhtmlEnvironment.updateMergedStyles

exist yet.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.lo_pool.LoPool.readBusy
documentation: Return info about the currently running LO servers

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.lo_pool.LoPool.chooseLO
documentation:
Choose and return the LO instance that will manage the current
request.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.lo_pool.LoPool.__call__
documentation:
Call one of our LO servers to convert or update, in this p_format,
the ODF pod result whose path is in p_resultName. Return LO's output
on stderr, which contains an error message if an error occurred and
is empty else.

Via this class, Appy can manage communication with several LibreOffice
(LO) servers.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Properties
documentation: Abstract base class for table and list properties

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.TableProperties

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.TableProperties.getWidth
documentation:
Return the table width as a css.Value object. p_attrs is a css.Styles
object containing parsed table attributes. If p_original is False,
p_self.wideAbove is not taken into account.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.TableProperties.getCellPadding
documentation:
CSS "border-spacing" is defined in p_value. This method gets the
final value, taking into account self.minCellPadding.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.TableProperties.getMargins
documentation:
Returns ODF properties allowing to define margins as specified by
self.margins or by CSS p_attrs. If no margin is defined, r_ is an
empty string.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.TableProperties.initStylesMapping
documentation:
If our special regular expressions are in use in parameters
p_ocw ("optimalColumnWidths") or p_dc ("distributeColumns"), we
must provide specific style mapping entries allowing to map CSS
attribute "table-layout" and its values to column modifiers
"optimize" or "distribute".

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.TableProperties.init
documentation:
Sets, on this p_class_, some static attributes related to column
width optimization.

In a styles mapping, the value @key "table" must be an instance of this
class.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.ListProperties

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.ListProperties.dumpStyle
documentation:
Returns the OpenDocument style definition corresponding to this
instance.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.ListProperties.getTextProperties
documentation: Allows to define text properties at level p_i
Base abstract class for defining properties of a XHTML list

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.BulletedProperties

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.BulletedProperties.getLevelAttributes
documentation: Dumps bullet-specific attributes for level p_i

In a styles mapping, the value @key "ul" must be an instance of this
class.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.NumberedProperties

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.NumberedProperties.getLevelAttributes
documentation: Dumps number-specific attributes for level p_i

In a styles mapping, the value @key "ol" must be an instance of this
class.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Style

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Style.__repr__
documentation: p_self's short string representation

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Style.getOdfAttributes
documentation:
Gets the ODF attributes corresponding to this style. p_attrs, when
given, are attributes of an XHTML tag.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Style.getOdfParentAttributes
documentation:
If style inheritance works, this method simply returns the attribute
"style:parent-style-name" allowing to define some style as child from
this one. If inheritance does not work (like for "table-cell"
styles), this method returns, in extenso, the parent ODF properties,
so they will be entirely copied into the child style.

Represents an ODF style. Either parsed from an ODF file or used for
dumping a style into an ODF file.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.GraphicalStyle

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.GraphicalStyle.getOdfAttributes
documentation: Returns p_self's attributes expressed as ODF

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.GraphicalStyle.asOdf
documentation: Returns p_self's representation as ODF code
Represents an ODF style of type "graphic"

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyle

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyle.rename
documentation: Give to this style a new, unique name

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyle.update
documentation:
If this style refers to other styles, update these references with
the new style names.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyle.reifyStartTag
documentation: Re-creates the ODF start tag corresponding to this style

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyle.reifyUsingStartTag
documentation: Re-creates the ODF style definition using this page style

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyle.__repr__
documentation: String representation for this page style
Represents a page style

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyles

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyles.add
documentation: Adds a newly parsed page p_style

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyles.init
documentation: Post-initialisation

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyles.getNewStyleDefinition
documentation: Produce the new style definition

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyles.getNewStyleUse
documentation: Produce the new style use

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageStyles.renameIn
documentation:
Adapts, in p_content, any mention (definition or usage) to this page
style according to its new unique name. If p_name is:
- "styles", p_content is the string content of styles.xml;
- "content", p_content is the string content of content.xml.
Stores all page styles found in a document

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageLayout

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageLayout.getFloat
documentation: Extract the float value from the string p_value

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageLayout.setProperties
documentation:
Sets properties of this page layout based on parsed p_attrs from tag
"page-layout-properties".

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageLayout.getWidth
documentation: Return, as a float, the page width in cm

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.PageLayout.getHeight
documentation: Return, as a float, the page height in cm
Represents a kind of page-level style

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Styles

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Styles.getParagraphStyleAtLevel
documentation:
Tries to find a style which has level p_level. Returns None if no
such style exists.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Styles.getStyle
documentation:
Gets the style that has this p_displayName. Returns None if not
found.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Styles.getStyles
documentation: Returns a list of all the styles of the given p_stylesType
A dict of styles

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.FontsInjector

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.FontsInjector.injectIn
documentation:
Injects, within every style found in a "styles.xml" file whose string
content is in p_stylesXml, the font as defined in p_self.font.
Allows to inject a font within all styles in a ODF document

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesEnvironment

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesEnvironment.propagateNamespaces
documentation:
Propagates the namespaces in all XML element definitions that are
used throughout POD.
Environment for the styles parser

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesParser
documentation: The styles parser

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Css2odf

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Css2odf.__init__
documentation:
The p_xhtmlParser from the current conversion from XHTML to ODT may
be required to fine-tune the conversion.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Css2odf.getOdf
documentation:
Compute, when possible, the ODF attribute, as a tuple (name, value)
corresponding to CSS attribute p_name having some p_value (and the
specific p_sub-value if p_value is multiple). If such an ODF
attribute is found, it is appended in this list of p_attributes.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Css2odf.isCombined
documentation:
Returns True if the CSS property p_name is a combined CSS property,
ie, a property representing a set of CSS properties.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.Css2odf.inTable
documentation: In the XHTML > ODT conversion, are we currently parsing a table ?
Allows to get a OpenDocument attribute from a CSS attribute

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.DynamicStyles

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.DynamicStyles.addListStyles
documentation: Add bullet-related styles in attr "styles_base"

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.DynamicStyles.add
documentation: Adds this p_style definition p_self's attribute named p_attr

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.DynamicStyles.injectIn
documentation:
Inject the styles generated and collected in p_self's attribute named
p_attr and inject them in p_content.
Styles generated on-the-fly by POD

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.addStyle
documentation:
Adds this p_style definition into this p_store (content.xml or
styles.xml).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.addStyleInstance
documentation:
Add the style named p_name to the style manager's defined styles, as
a Style instance.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.addGraphicalStyle
documentation:
Creates and add a style of type "graphic" within the dynamically
generated styles.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.getStyleHash
documentation:
Returns a string uniquely representing the given set of ODF
attributes p_odfAttrs that will be used to create/retrieve a style to
be applied to the ODF element corresponding to p_xhtmlElem.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.getStyleFamily
documentation: Returns the ODF family style corresponding to p_xhtmlElem

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.generateStyleName
documentation: Generates a new style name

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.getStyleName
documentation:
Return the ODF style name for the style that will be applied to ODF
element corresponding to the given XHTML element (p_xhtmlElem). We
have already computed attributes for the ODF style in p_odfAttrs. If
a style has already been generated by this styles generator, for this
tag and with this combination of attributes, we simply return its
name. Else, we note that we must generate a new style; we compute a
new (incremental) name for it and we return this name.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.flattenOdfAttributes
documentation:
Produce a string from the list of (name, value) pairs in
p_odfAttrs.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.isTextProperty
documentation: Is property p_name a text property ?

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.getStylesStore
documentation:
Where must a style related to this p_tag be generated ? In
content.xml or in styles.xml ?
This method generates most of the tags. If no specific method is found for
some tag, this method is used.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.get_any
documentation: Generates a (or returns an existing) paragraph style

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.get_td
documentation: Generates a table cell style

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.get
documentation:
Generates a custom style when relevant. p_baseStyle is a Style object
that could have been found from a style mapping. In this case, the
generated style will get it as parent style.

Analyse, for a given XHTML tag, its attributes (including CSS attributes
within the "style" attribute) and possibly generate a custom style.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.checkStylesAdequation
documentation: Checks that p_odtStyle may be used for style p_htmlStyle

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.addStyleEntry
documentation:
Adds, in dict p_stylesMapping (the output of m_checkStylesMapping
below), a p_key:p_value entry.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.checkStylesMapping
documentation:
Checks that the given p_stylesMapping is correct, and returns the
internal representation of it.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.styleMatch
documentation:
p_matchingAttrs is a dict of attributes corresponding to some style
(or can be None). This method returns True if p_xhtmlElem has the
the winning (name, value) pairs that match those in p_matchingAttrs.
Note that ALL attrs in p_matchingAttrs must be present on
p_xhtmlElem.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.getMatchingStyle
documentation:
p_styles is a value from a styles mapping as transformed by
m_checkStylesMapping above. If it represents a list of styles
(see case (ii) in m_checkStylesMapping), this method must return the
relevant Style instance from this list, depending on CSS attributes
found on p_xhtmlElem.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.getStyleFromMapping
documentation:
If p_stylesMapping contains a style corresponding to p_xhtmlElem, it
returns it. This method takes care of meta-elems like "para"
representing several elements. This method is recursive: when called
to get a style from a meta-element, it is done via p_elem.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.getLoStyle
documentation:
Gets the LibreOffice style (from self.styles) corresponding to some
style p_name, if it exists.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.findStyle
documentation:
Finds the ODT style that must be applied to this p_xhtmlElem (as a
xhtml2odt:HtmlElement object).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.setXhtmlParser
documentation: Store the p_xhtmlParser if a XHTML > ODT conversion is ongoing

Reads the paragraph styles from styles.xml within an ODT file, and
updates styles.xml with some predefined POD styles.

modules.name: py3.appy.data
documentation:
This folder contains copies of external, "authentic" data, stored as text
files, like ISO 3166-1 country codes. In this package, corresponding Python
classes are available for accessing the data in the text files.

classes.name: py3.appy.data.Languages

methods.name: py3.appy.data.Languages.parseFile
documentation:
Parses the language codes and names in the ISO file and puts them in
self.languageCodes, self.languageNames and self.nativeNames.

methods.name: py3.appy.data.Languages.exists
documentation: Is p_code a valid 2-digits language code ?

methods.name: py3.appy.data.Languages.get
documentation: Returns information about the language whose code is p_code

This class gives access to the language codes and names as standardized
by ISO-639. The file has been downloaded in July 2009 from
http://www.loc.gov/standards/iso639-2/ascii_8bits.html (UTF-8 version)

classes.name: py3.appy.data.Countries

methods.name: py3.appy.data.Countries.get
documentation:
Returns the single existing instance of this class in
p_class_.instance. If it does noe exist yet, it is created.

methods.name: py3.appy.data.Countries.exists
documentation: Is p_code a valid 2-digits country code ?

This class gives access to the country codes and names as standardized by
ISO 3166-1. The file has been downloaded in March 2011 from
http://www.iso.org/iso/country_codes/iso_3166_code_lists.htm
(first line has been removed).

classes.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext._showSwitchOptions
documentation: In some situations, switch options are not to be shown

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext._getSwitchOptions
documentation:
Returns a tuple (options, present), where:
- "options" is the list of values for the "switch" widget (=the list
  of possible contexts for the user);
- "present" is a boolean value indicating if the current
  authentication context is in this list.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext._maySwitchTo
documentation: Is p_option a valid option to switch to ?

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext._check
documentation:
Returns True if there is no problem with the current configuration
related to the authentication context.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext._setOnGuard
documentation: Set, on the the p_guard, authentication-p_ctx-related attributes

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext.isMandatory
documentation:
When authentication contexts are activated, is the user forced to
choose one ?

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext.switchContext
documentation: Is the user allowed to switch context once logged ?

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext.getContexts
documentation:
Returns the application-specific authentication contexts, as a list
of tuples (s_context, s_name). s_context is a short string that
identifies the context, while s_name is a human-readable name that
will be shown in the UI.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext.getDefaultContext
documentation:
Returns the default context among contexts as returned by
m_getContexts.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext.getName
documentation: Returns the name of some given p_context
This method must not be overridden

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext.getObject
documentation:
If an authentication context corresponds to an object, this method
must be overridden to return the object corresponding to the given
p_context. Then, the object is available as attribute "authObject"
on the guard.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.context.AuthenticationContext.switch
documentation: Switches from one authentication context to another

When an application uses an authentication context, its users, when
logging in, must, besides their login and password, choose a context
among possible authentication contexts. Then, your application has access
to the chosen context via the handler attribute "authContext".

If you want to use authentication contexts in your Appy application, you
must create a class that inherits from this one and overrides some of its
methods (see below). Then, in your Config instance set an instance of
your class in attribute config.security.authContext.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.CsvOptions

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.CsvOptions.asString
documentation: Returns options as a string, ready-to-be sent to the Converter

When the POD result is of type CSV, use this class to define CSV
options.

classes.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.__init__
documentation:
Base on a document template (whose path is in p_template), which is
an ODT or ODS file containing special expressions and statements
written in Python, this renderer generates an ODT file (whose path is
in p_result), that is a copy of the template whose expressions and
statements have been replaced with the objects defined in the
p_context.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.reinit
documentation:
Re-initialise this renderer (p_self) for recycling him and produce
another p_result with another p_context.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.clone
documentation:
Creates another Renderer instance, similar to p_self, but for
rendering a different p_result based on another p_context and
p_template, with possibly different other p_params. Any parameter not
being in p_params but listed in Renderer.cloneAttributes will be
copied from p_self to the clone.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.formatFinalizeFunction
documentation: Standardize "finalize function" p_fun as a list of functions

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.enablePpp
documentation:
Activate the POD post-processor, which is implemented as a series of
UNO commands.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.getCompleteContext
documentation:
Create and return a complete context, from the one given by the pod
developer (p_context).

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.createPodParser
documentation:
Creates the parser with its environment for parsing the given
p_odtFile (content.xml or styles.xml). p_context is given by the pod
user, while p_inserts depends on the ODT file we must parse.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.createParsers
documentation:
Creates parsers for content.xml and styles.xml and store them,
respectively, in p_self.contentParser and p_self.stylesParser.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.renderXhtml
documentation:
Method that can be used (under the name "xhtml") into a POD template
for converting a chunk of XHTML content (p_s) into a chunk of ODF
content.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.renderText
documentation:
Renders the pure text p_s in the ODF result. For every carriage
return character present in p_s, a new paragraph is created in the
ODF result.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.importDocument
documentation: Implements the POD statement "do... from document"

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.importImage
documentation:
While m_importDocument allows to import a document or image via a
"do ... from document" statement, method m_importImage allows to
import an image anchored as a char via a POD expression
":image(...)", having almost the same parameters.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.getResolvedNamespaces
documentation: Gets a context where mainly used namespaces have been resolved

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.importPod
documentation: Implements the POD statement "do... from pod"

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.importCell
documentation:
Creates a chunk of ODF code ready to be dumped as a table cell
containing this p_content and styled with this p_style.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.drawShape
documentation: Renders a shape within a POD result

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer._insertBreak
documentation: Inserts a page or column break into the result

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.prepareFolders
documentation:
Ensure p_self.result is correct and create, when relevant, the temp
folder for preparing it.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.patchMetadata
documentation:
Declares, in META-INF/manifest.xml, images or files included via the
"do... from document" statements if any, and patch meta.xml (field
"title").

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.run
documentation: Renders the result
Public interface

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.getStyles
documentation: Returns a dict of the styles that are defined into the template

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.setStylesMapping
documentation:
Establishes a correspondence between, on one hand, CSS styles or
XHTML tags that will be found inside XHTML content given to POD,
and, on the other hand, ODT styles found into the template.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.getTemplateType
documentation:
Identifies the type of this POD p_template (ods or odt). If
p_template is a string, it is a file name: simply get its extension.
Else, it is a binary file in a BytesIO instance: seek the MIME type
from the first bytes.

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.callLibreOffice
documentation: Call LibreOffice in server mode to convert or update the result

methods.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.finalize
documentation:
Re-zip the result and potentially call LibreOffice if target format
is not among self.templateTypes or if forceOoCall is True.

class_variables.name: py3.appy.pod.renderer.Renderer.templateTypes
documentation: Types of POD templates
Produce (="render") a document from a template

modules.name: py3.appy.server.backup
documentation: This file is responsible for perfoming the backup of a Appy site

classes.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Config

methods.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Config.check
documentation: Checks the backup configuration
Backup configuration

classes.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Backup

methods.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Backup.isFull
documentation: Returns True if the backup must be a full backup

methods.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Backup.log
documentation: Logs this m_message and adds it to p_self.messages

methods.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Backup.logDone
documentation: Logs the "done" message, with the time elapsed since p_start

methods.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Backup.runCommand
documentation: Runs and logs a commmand made of p_args

methods.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Backup.runMethod
documentation:
Runs the custom method as defined in the backup config. Returns True
if the method did not raise an exception.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Backup.copyDatabase
documentation: Copy the .fs file to the <backup folder>/zodb, using rsync

methods.name: py3.appy.server.backup.Backup.copyFiles
documentation:

documentation:
Returns the complete URI for accessing the LDAP, ie
"ldap[s]://some.ldap.server:389".

documentation:
Gets the filter values required to perform a query for finding user
corresponding to p_login in the LDAP, or all users if p_login is
None.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.Config.getUserAttributes
documentation:
Gets the attributes we want to get from the LDAP for characterizing
a user.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.Config.setAppyValue
documentation:
Convert LDAP value found at key p_ldapName in p_ldapData to an
Appy-compliant value and store it, if found, in p_appyData at key
p_appyName.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.Config.getUserParams
documentation:
Formats the user-related p_ldapData retrieved from the LDAP, as a
dict of params usable for creating or updating the corresponding
Appy user.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.Config.setLocalUser
documentation:
Creates or updates the local User instance corresponding to a LDAP
user from the LDAP, having p_login. Its other attributes are in
p_attrs and, when relevant, its password is in p_password. This
method r_eturns a 2-tuple containing:
* the local User instance;
* the status of the operation:
  - "created" if the instance has been created,
  - "updated" if at least one data from p_attrs is different from the
    one stored on the existing User instance;
  - None else.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.Config.applyLoginTransform
documentation: Applies, when relevant, a transform to p_login

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.Config.getUser
documentation:
Returns a local User instance corresponding to a LDAP user if p_login
and p_password correspond to a valid LDAP user.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.Config.updateUserStates
documentation:
Browse local users and activate or deactivate some of them, based on
freshly queried LDAP users from p_ldapUsers. Update p_counts of users
(attributes "deactivated" and "reactivated").

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.Config.synchronizeUsers
documentation:
Synchronizes the local User copies with this LDAP user base. Returns
a message with details about the operation.

Parameters for authenticating users to an LDAP server. This class is
used by Appy apps. For a pure, appy-independent LDAP connector,
see the class LdapConnector below.

classes.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.LdapConnector

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.LdapConnector.log
documentation: Logs via a Appy tool if available

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.LdapConnector.connect
documentation:
Connects to the LDAP server using p_login and p_password as
credentials. If the connection succeeds, a server object is created
in self.server and tuple (True, None) is returned. Else, tuple
(False, errorMessage) is returned.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.LdapConnector.getFilter
documentation:
Builds and returns a LDAP filter based on p_values, a tuple of
(name, value) expressions logically AND-ed. If p_customPart is given,
it represents and additional expression that will be AND-ed as
well.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.ldap.LdapConnector.search
documentation:
Performs a query in the LDAP at node p_baseDn, with the given
p_scope. p_filter is a LDAP filter that constraints the search. It
can be computed from a list of tuples (value, name) by method
m_getFilter. p_attributes is the list of attributes that we will
retrieve from the LDAP. If None, all attributes will be retrieved.
This class manages the communication with a LDAP server

retrieve, within every HTTP request from the reverse proxy, information
about the currently logged user: login, groups and roles.

classes.name: py3.appy.deploy.repository.Repository

methods.name: py3.appy.deploy.repository.Repository.getDownloadCommand
documentation:
Return a tuple (v_command, v_localFolder). v_command is the complete
command allowing to download the distant code into this local
p_folder (p_folder being generally a site's "lib" folder);
v_localFolder is the folder created within p_folder.
2 commands can be issued on a distant repository: "download" and "update".
Names for those commands are purposedly chosen to be technology neutral.
"download" | refers to the initial import of data from the distant
| repository. For git, it is implemented by the "git clone"
| command; for Subversion, it is implemented by "svn checkout".
"udate"    | refers to an update to an already imported codebase. It is
| implemented by commands "git pull" or "svn up".
Consequently, every concrete Repository class must implement the 2
following methods, allowing to produce the complete specific commands for
performing these 2 operations on the distant repository.

methods.name: py3.appy.deploy.repository.Repository.getUpdateCommand
documentation:
Return a tuple (v_command, v_localFolder). v_command is the complete
command allowing to update the distant code that was previously
imported in p_folder (p_folder being generally a site's "lib"
folder); v_localFolder is the inner folder, within p_folder,
containing the code.

methods.name: py3.appy.server.handler.MethodsCache.call
documentation:
Call p_method on some p_o(bject). m_method can be an instance method
on p_o; it can also be a static method. In this latter case, p_o is
the tool and the static method, defined in p_class_, will be called
with the tool as unique arg.

If the method result is already in the cache, it will simply be
returned. Else, the method will be executed, its result will be
stored in the cache and returned.

If p_cache is False, caching is disabled and the method is always
executed.

A dict of method results, used to avoid executing more than once the same
method, while handling a request. Every handler implements such a
cache.

Override this method to analyse arguments that were parsed an stored
in self.args:
* check their validity;
* potentially apply some computation on it and store, on p_self, new

If this method detects an error within self.args, abort program
execution by calling m_exit (see below).

modules.name: py3.appy.ui.validate
documentation: Validation-related stuff

classes.name: py3.appy.ui.validate.Validator

methods.name: py3.appy.ui.validate.Validator.intraFieldValidation
documentation:
This method performs field-specific validation for every field from
the page being created or edited. For every field whose validation
generates an error, we add an entry in p_self.errors. For every
field, we add in p_self.values an entry with the "ready-to-store"
field value.

Il p_fields are given, we use them instead of getting currently
edited fields on the current page for p_self.o. ie, p_fields can be
fields from a switch.

methods.name: py3.appy.ui.validate.Validator.interFieldValidation
documentation:
This method is called when individual validation of all fields
succeeds (when editing or creating an object, see
m_intraFieldValidation). Then, this method performs inter-field
validation. This way, the user must first correct individual fields
before being confronted to potential inter-field validation
errors.

methods.name: py3.appy.ui.validate.Validator.addMessage
documentation:
Used by m_getGroupedErrors, this method adds, in p_r, the error
p_message about a field whose label is p_label. Indexes of messages
already stored in p_r are in dict p_messages.

methods.name: py3.appy.ui.validate.Validator.getGroupedErrors
documentation:
Returns the list of all errors, grouped by message: if the same error
message concerns several fields, a single entry is inserted in the
list.

methods.name: py3.appy.ui.validate.Validator.gotoEdit
documentation:
Brings the user to the edit page for p_self.o. This method takes care
of not carrying any password value. Unlike m_goto above, there is no
HTTP redirect here: we execute directly PX "edit" and we return the
result.

methods.name: py3.appy.ui.validate.Validator.run
documentation: Runs all validation steps

methods.name: py3.appy.ui.validate.Validator.getJsErrors
documentation:
Generates the definition of a JavaScript associative array
containing all errors collected in p_handler.validator.errors.
Class responsible for validating data encoded by the user in the UI

In the context of a replacement, this method returns p_self.repl,
that will replace the matched p_self.keyword. In the context of a
simple grep, the method returns the matched content, left untouched.
In both contexts, p_counts about matches are updated.

methods.name: py3.appy.bin.ogrep.Grep.grepFileContent
documentation:
Finds self.keyword among p_tempFolder/content.xml and
p_tempFolder/styles.xml, whose content is in p_contents and was
extracted from file @p_path, and return the number of matches. If
Match objects must be built, p_self.matches are updated accordingly.

If self.repl is there, and if there is at least one match, the method
replaces self.keyword by self.repl for all matches, re-zips the ODF
file whose parts are in p_tempFolder and overwrites the original file
in p_path.

classes.name: py3.appy.ui.svg.Config
documentation: Configuration of SVG icons
Just like CSS and JS files, SVG images are loaded in RAM at Appy server
startup. This is not just for performance reasons: it makes it possible to
patch them when loaded.
The SVG file may contain special markers of the form |<name>|. If <name>
corresponds to one of the attributes of class SvgConfig below, it will be
replaced with the attribute value of the SvgConfig instance as loaded in
the global config, in config.ui.svg.

classes.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.init
documentation: Lazy initialisation

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.copyPerms
documentation: Gets a deep copy of this state's permissions dict

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.getRole
documentation:
p_role can be the name of a role or a Role instance. If it is the
name of a role, this method returns self.usedRoles[role] if it
exists, or creates a Role instance, puts it in self.usedRoles and
returns it else. If it is a Role instance, the method stores it in
self.usedRoles if it is not in it yet and returns it.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.standardizeRoles
documentation:
This method converts, within self.permissions, every role to a
Role instance. Every used role is stored in self.usedRoles.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.getRolesFor
documentation:
Gets the roles that are granted p_permission on this state. r_ is a
dict ~{s_roleName: Role}.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.addRoles
documentation:
Adds p_roles in self.permissions. p_roles can be a role name, a Role
instance or a list of names and/or Role instances. If p_permissions
is specified, roles are added to those permissions only. Else, roles
are added for every permission within self.permissions.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.removeRoles
documentation:
Removes p_roleNames within dict self.permissions. If p_permissions is
specified, removal is restricted to those permissions. Else, removal
occurs throughout the whole dict self.permissions.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.setRoles
documentation:
Sets p_rolesNames for p_permissions if not empty, for every
permission in self.permissions else.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.replaceRole
documentation:
Replaces p_oldRoleName by p_newRoleName. If p_permissions is
specified, the replacement is restricted to those permissions. Else,
replacements apply to the whole dict self.permissions.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.copyRoles
documentation:
Overrides p_destPermission's roles with (a deep copy of)
p_sourcePermission's roles.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.workflow.state.State.isIsolated
documentation:
Returns True if, from this state, we cannot reach another state.
Modifying a workflow for getting a state with auto-transitions only
is a common technique for disabling a state in a workflow.
Represents a workflow state

classes.name: py3.appy.model.utils.Object

methods.name: py3.appy.model.utils.Object.__init__
documentation: Warning - This class is particular: attribute access has been modified in
such a way that AttributeError is never raised.
Example:                 o = Object(a=1)
>>>                      (None is returned)
>>> hasattr(o, 'a')
>>> True
>>> hasattr(o, 'b')
>>> True                 It's like if any attribute was defined on it
>>> 'a' in o
>>> True
>>> 'b' in o
>>> False
While this behaviour may be questionable and dangerous, it can be very
practical for objects that can potentially contain any attribute (like an
object representing HTTP request parameters or form values). Code getting
attribute values can be shorter, freed from verbose "hasattr" calls.
Conclusion: use this class at your own risk, if you know what you are
doing (isn't it the Python spirit?)

classes.name: py3.appy.model.utils.Cloner

methods.name: py3.appy.model.utils.Cloner.clone
documentation: Create and return a clone for this Base p_field
By default, appy.model.base.Base fields are inherited by any other Appy
class (Tool, Translation, etc) as well as any Appy class from any app.
This may pose problems. Firstly, the container class as mentioned in such
commmon Field objects will be wrong. Functionalities based on it, like
recomputing an index from the Admin Zone > Model page will produce wrong
results. Moreover, m_update methods from app classes may update Field
objects: the develper may no realize that his changes will be propagated
to all classes.
Consequently, when generating the model for an app, Base fields must be
cloned in every class.
Creates Field objects as clones of Base Field objects

This can be useful if, when changing a workflow, one wants to remove

disabled state.

modules.name: py3.appy.model.searches.initiators
documentation: Initiators for searches whose results are shown in popups

classes.name: py3.appy.model.searches.initiators.Initiator
documentation:
When a search is rendered in a popup, the "initiator", in the main page,
can be:
* (a) some object, in view or edit mode, displaying a given Ref field
      for which the popup is used to select one or more objects to link;
* (b) some class for which we must create an instance from a template;
      the popup is used to select such a template object.

This class is the abstract class for 2 concrete initiator classes:
RefInitiator (for case a) and TemplateInitiator (for case b).

A PARTICULAR PURPOSE. See the GNU General Public License for more details.
You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License along with

"first name" / "name".

classes.name: py3.appy.model.totals.Total

methods.name: py3.appy.model.totals.Total.getStyled
documentation:
If p_tag is passed ("th" or "td"), return the complete table cell
containing p_self.value, with a potential p_self.style applied. If
p_tag is None, it only returns the style attribute corresponding to
p_self.style.

Represents the computation of a specific total that will be aggregated
from a series of cells within a list of objects.

documentation: Create a Running* object containing Total objects

methods.name: py3.appy.model.totals.Totals.updateAll
documentation:
Called before rendering a field value, this method updates all totals
being currently computed.

If you want to add column or rows representing totals computed from lists
of objects (List/Dict rows, Search results), specify it via Totals
objects (see doc on the using class).

classes.name: py3.appy.model.totals.RunningRows

methods.name: py3.appy.model.totals.RunningRows.__init__
documentation: Every key is the ID of a Totals instance. Every value is an object storing
one attribute per field whose total must be computed (Totals.fields). So,
for each such field, this object stores O.<fieldName> = <Total instance>.

Returns, within p_self, the Total object corresponding to the Totals

methods.name: py3.appy.model.totals.RunningRows.update

Every time a cell is encountered while rendering a given field value
within a table or list of objects for which totals must be computed,
this method is called, if required, to update p_self's running
totals.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.totals.RunningRows.getCellFor
documentation:
Generates, as a complete "td" tag, the cell corresponding to this
p_col(umn) within this total p_row (=Totals object).

Represents a bunch of Total objects being currently computed, for totals

modules.name: py3.appy.model.loader
documentation: Loads and returns a Model instance

classes.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader

methods.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.log
documentation: Logs p_message to p_self.logger if it exists, to stdout else

methods.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.determineMetaClass
py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.complete
documentation:
After the model loader has loaded the app's model (m_run), Appy base
classes (see Model.baseClasses) may have been redefined or not at the
app level. In order to have a complete model in self.classes, we must
add all base classes not being redefined at the app level.

We must also load standard workflows into self.workflows.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.updateRefs
documentation:
For "base" and "over" classes, Refs may still point to "base"
classes. For example, Tool.users may point to appy.model.user.User
instead of "over" class someApp.user.User. This method updates Refs
accordingly.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.linkWorkflows
documentation:
Associate classes and workflows at the model level, based on static
attributes "workflows" defined on classes, and check that such
classes are really workflows.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.updateTranslations
documentation:
Injects, on class Translation, a field for every defined i18n
label. Returns the total number of i18n labels.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.getAppFolders
documentation:
Returns the list of folders and sub-folders, within the app folder,
where to look for Appy classes and workflows.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.getAppModules
documentation:
Returns the imported Python modules corresponding to these
p_folders.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.loader.Loader.run
documentation: Loads the model

Reads the application model, completes it with the base Appy model, and
returns a appy.model.Model object.

methods.name: py3.appy.model.tool.Tool.computeHomePage
documentation: Compute the page that is shown to the user when he hits the app

methods.name: py3.appy.model.tool.Tool.computeHomeObject
documentation:
The concept of "home object" is the object where the user must "be",
even if he is "nowhere". For example, if the user is on a search
screen, there is no contextual object (the tool excepted). In this
case, if we have a home object for him, we will use it as contextual
object, and its portlet menu will nevertheless appear: the user will
not have the feeling of being lost.
