functions.name: py3.appy.utils.path.copyFolder
documentation:
Copies the content of folder p_source to folder p_dest. p_dest is
created, with intermediary subfolders if required. If p_cleanDest is
True, it removes completely p_dest if it existed. Else, content of
p_source will be added to possibly existing content in p_dest, excepted

documentation: Functions for (un)zipping files

functions.name: py3.appy.utils.zip.subUnzip
documentation:
Unzip the zip file named p_zipName into this p_folder, by creating,
within p_folder, a sub-folder with a unique name, into which p_zipName
content will be unzippped. p_zipName is deleted at the end of the
operation.

functions.name: py3.appy.utils.zip.unzip
documentation:
Unzips file p_f into p_folder. p_f can be anything accepted by the
zipfile.ZipFile constructor. p_folder must exist.

functions.name: py3.appy.utils.zip.zip
documentation:
Zips the content of p_folder into the zip file whose (preferably)
absolute filename is p_f. If p_odf is True, p_folder is considered to
contain the standard content of an ODF file (content.xml,...). In this
case, some rules must be respected while building the zip (see below).
If p_encode is True, we ensure the name of every file in the zip is
encoded with encoding CP437.

methods.name: py3.appy.utils.css.Styles.parse
documentation:
Returns a list of CSS (name, value) pairs (or a dict if p_asDict is
True), parsed from p_value, which holds the content of a HTML "style"

methods.name: py3.appy.utils.css.Styles.__init__
documentation:
Analyses styles as found in p_attrs and/or deduced from XHTML element
p_elem, and sets, for every found style, an attribute on self. This
constructor can also be used to build a Styles instance from
another Styles instance, given in p_other. A Styles instance
can also be initialised from p_kwargs representing CSS attributes.
CSS styles defined in p_kwargs override those in p_other, that, in
turn, override any existing value. Within p_kwargs, CSS attribute
names must not contain any dash (it would produce illegal Python
code).

methods.name: py3.appy.utils.css.Styles.get
documentation:
Gets the value (as a Value instance) corresponding to the CSS
attribute p_name. If p_name is ommitted, it returns all CSS
attributes as a dict. This method allows to use the original CSS
attribute names (possibly containing dashes). If this Styles
instance does not store such an attribute, no error is raised:
None is returned.

methods.name: py3.appy.utils.css.Styles.addClass

methods.name: py3.appy.utils.css.Styles.getClass

This method gives the path where to dump the content of the document

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.doc_importers.DocImporter.moveFile
documentation:
In the case "self.at" was used, we may want to move the file at
self.at within the ODT result in self.importPath (for images) or do
nothing (for docs). In the latter case, the file to import stays
at self.at, and is not copied into self.importPath. So the previously
computed self.importPath is not used at all.

Base class used for importing external content into a pod template (an
image, another pod template, another odt document...

p_result can be the aobsolute path to the result to produce or only a
file extension indicating the format of the file to produce. This
method returns this format.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getCustomFunctions
documentation:
Compiles and executes the Python p_script and returns a dict
containing its namespace.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getFilePath
documentation:
Returns the absolute path of p_filePath. In fact, it returns a
tuple with some URL version of the path for LO as the first element
and the absolute path as the second element.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getResultFilter
documentation:
Based on the result type, identifies which OO filter to use for the
document conversion.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getResultUrl
documentation:
Returns the path of the result file in the format needed by LO. If
p_result is a file path, it will be used as base. Else, it is a file
extension (=the "result type"): computing the complete path will be
done as follows.

If the result type and the input type are the same (ie the user wants
to refresh indexes or some other action and not perform a real
conversion), the result file will be named
                <inputFileName>.res.<resultType>

Else, the result file will be named like the input file but with a
different extension:
                <inputFileName>.<resultType>

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.props
documentation:
Create a UNO-compliant tuple of properties, from tuple p_properties
containing sub-tuples (s_propertyName, value).

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getConfig
documentation:
Returns a node within the LO configuration, stored at p_path. If the
objective is to update it, set p_update to True.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getVersion
documentation: Returns the LO version

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.connect
documentation: Connects to LibreOffice

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.getColumnModifiers
documentation:
Returns the elements allowing to know if we must optimize or
distribute table columns.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.converter.Converter.processTableColumns
documentation:
Process column widths for this p_table: either p_optimize them or
p_distribute them evenly (one of these 2 params must be True). Note
that:
* the "optimize" algorithm dos not work properly in all cases, unless
  algorithm "distribute" is executed beforehand. So, in short:
           optimize = distribute + optimize
* if both p_optimize and p_distribute are specified, the p_optimize
  operation will be performed and will produce the final result.
  Indeed, even if a "distribute" preamble operation is performed, it
  will not be the final result.

modules.name: py2.appy.bin.checkldap
documentation: This script allows to check a LDAP connection.

classes.name: py2.appy.bin.checkldap.LdapTester
documentation:
Usage: python checkldap.py ldapUri login password base attrs filter scope

ldapUri is, for example, "ldap://127.0.0.1:389"
login is the login user DN, ie: "cn=gdy,o=geezteem"
password is the password for this login
base is the base DN where to perform the search, ie "ou=hr,o=GeezTeem"
attrs is a comma-separated list of attrs we will retrieve in the LDAP,
      ie "uid,login"
filter is the query filter, ie "(&(attr1=Geez*)(status=OK))"
scope is the scope of the search, and can be:
  BASE     To search the object itself on base
  ONELEVEL To search base's immediate children
  SUBTREE  To search base and all its descendants

Returns a string uniquely representing the given set of ODF
attributes p_odfAttrs that will be used to create/retrieve a style to

documentation: Returns the ODF family style corresponding to p_xhtmlElem

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.generateStyleName
documentation: Generates a new style name

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.getStyleName
documentation:
Return the ODF style name for the style that will be applied to ODF
element corresponding to the given XHTML element (p_xhtmlElem). We
have already computed attributes for the ODF style in p_odfAttrs. If
a style has already been generated by this styles generator, for this
tag and with this combination of attributes, we simply return its
name. Else, we note that we must generate a new style; we compute a
new (incremental) name for it and we return this name.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.flattenOdfAttributes
documentation:
Produce a string from the list of (name, value) pairs in
p_odfAttrs.

This method generates most of the tags. If no specific method is found for
some tag, this method is used.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.get_td
documentation: Generates a table cell style

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesGenerator.get
documentation:
Generates a custom style when relevant. p_baseStyle is a Style
instance that could have been found from a style mapping. In this
case, the generated style will get it as parent style.

Analyse, for a given XHTML tag, its attributes (including CSS attributes
within the "style" attribute) and possibly generate a custom style.

classes.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.checkStylesAdequation
documentation: Checks that p_odtStyle may be used for style p_htmlStyle

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.addStyleEntry
documentation:
Adds, in dict p_stylesMapping (the output of m_checkStylesMapping
below), a p_key:p_value entry.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.checkStylesMapping
documentation:
Checks that the given p_stylesMapping is correct, and returns the
internal representation of it.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.styleMatch
documentation:
p_matchingAttrs is a dict of attributes corresponding to some style
(or can be None). This method returns True if p_xhtmlElem has the
the winning (name, value) pairs that match those in p_matchingAttrs.
Note that ALL attrs in p_matchingAttrs must be present on
p_xhtmlElem.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.getMatchingStyle
documentation:
p_styles is a value from a styles mapping as transformed by
m_checkStylesMapping above. If it represents a list of styles
(see case (ii) in m_checkStylesMapping), this method must return the
relevant Style instance from this list, depending on CSS attributes
found on p_xhtmlElem.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.getStyleFromMapping
documentation:
If p_stylesMapping contains a style corresponding to p_xhtmlElem, it
returns it. This method takes care of meta-elems like "para"
representing several elements. This method is recursive: when called
to get a style from a meta-element, it is done via p_elem.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.getLoStyle
documentation:
Gets the LibreOffice style (from self.styles) corresponding to some
style p_name, if it exists.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.findStyle
documentation:
Finds the ODT style that must be applied to XHTML p_elem (as a
   xhtml2odt:HtmlElement instance).

   The global styles mapping is in self.stylesMapping; the local styles
   mapping is in p_localStylesMapping.

   Here are the places where we will search, ordered by
   priority (highest first):
   (1) local styles mapping (CSS style in "class" attr)
   (2)         "            (HTML elem)
   (3) global styles mapping (CSS style in "class" attr)
   (4)          "            (HTML elem)
   (5) ODT style that has the same name as CSS style in "class" attr
   (6) Predefined pod-specific ODT style that has the same name as
       CSS style in "class" attr
   (7) ODT style that has the same outline level as HTML elem
   (8) a default Appy ODT style

After this step, we have (or not) a base ODT style. In a final step we
will analyse XHTML attributes (including CSS attributes within the
"style" attribute) to get more elements and possibly generate, via the
styles generator define hereabove, a custom style based on the base
ODT style.

methods.name: py2.appy.pod.styles_manager.StylesManager.setXhtmlParser
documentation: Store the p_xhtmlParser if a XHTML > ODT conversion is ongoing

Reads the paragraph styles from styles.xml within an ODT file, and
updates styles.xml with some predefined POD styles.

classes.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles

methods.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles.__init__
documentation:
Analyses styles as found in p_attrs and/or deduced from XHTML element
p_elem, and sets, for every found style, an attribute on self. This
constructor can also be used to build a CssStyles instance from
another CssStyles instance, given in p_other. A CssStyles instance
can also be initialised from p_kwargs representing CSS attributes.
CSS styles defined in p_kwargs override those in p_other, that, in
turn,override any existing value. Within p_kwargs, CSS attribute
names must not contain any dash (it would produce illegal Python
code).

methods.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles.get
documentation:
Gets the value (as a CssValue instance) corresponding to the CSS
attribute p_name. If p_name is ommitted, it returns all CSS
attributes as a dict. This method allows to use the original CSS
attribute names (possibly containing dashes). If this CssStyles
instance does not store such an attribute, no error is raised:
None is returned.

methods.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles.addClass
documentation:
Add CSS class(es) p_value (as a string) to self.classes. p_value can
hold several CSS space-separated classes.

methods.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles.deleteClass
documentation: Remove CSS class(es) in p_value from self.classes

methods.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles.getClass
documentation:
Returns the name of the CSS class(es) defined in p_self.classes, or
None if no class is defined. If p_last is True, and several classes
are defined, only the last one is returned.

methods.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles.hasClass
documentation: Is CSS class p_css defined ?

methods.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles.add

also return it. p_name can be the original name of the CSS attribute
(possibly containing dashes).

methods.name: py2.appy.shared.css.CssStyles.delete
documentation:
Removes CSS attribute p_name from self, or only p_value for

modules.name: py2.appy.gen.validate
documentation: Validation-related stuff

classes.name: py2.appy.gen.validate.Validator

methods.name: py2.appy.gen.validate.Validator.intraFieldValidation
documentation:
This method performs field-specific validation for every field from
the page that is being created or edited. For every field whose
validation generates an error, we add an entry in p_self.errors. For
every field, we add in p_self.values an entry with the
"ready-to-store" field value.

Il p_fields are given, we use them instead of getting currently
edited fields on the current page for p_self.obj. ie, p_fields can be
fields from a switch.

methods.name: py2.appy.gen.validate.Validator.interFieldValidation
documentation:
This method is called when individual validation of all fields
succeeds (when editing or creating an object, see
m_intraFieldValidation). Then, this method performs inter-field
validation. This way, the user must first correct individual fields
before being confronted to potential inter-field validation
errors.

methods.name: py2.appy.gen.validate.Validator.addMessage
documentation:
Used by m_getErrors, this method adds, in p_r, the error p_message
about a field whose label is p_label. Indexes of messages already
stored in p_r are in dict p_messages.

methods.name: py2.appy.gen.validate.Validator.getGroupedErrors
documentation:
Returns the list of all errors, grouped by message: if the same error
message concerns several fields, a single entry is inserted in the
list.

methods.name: py2.appy.gen.validate.Validator.getJsErrors
documentation:
Generates the definition of a JavaScript associative array
containing all errors collected in p_self.errors.
Class responsible for validating data encoded by the user in the UI

This method must not be overridden

methods.name: py2.appy.gen.authenticate.AuthenticationContext.getObject
documentation:
If an authentication context corresponds to an object, this method
must be overridden to return the object corresponding to the given
p_context. Then, the object is available as attribute "authObject"
on the request.

When an application uses an authentication context, its users, when
logging in, must, besides their login and password, choose a context
among possible authentication contexts. Then, your application has access
to the chosen context via the request attribute "authContext".

If you want to use authentication contexts in your Appy application, you
must create a class that inherits from this one and overrides some of its
methods (see below). Then, in your Config instance (appy.gen.Config),
set an instance of your class in attribute "authContext".

p_value is the new value we want to index in the catalog, for a Ref

Indeed, some values may not be acceptable for the catalog: if it it an
empty list, string, None or an empty tuple, for example, instead of using
it, the catalog will keep the previously catalogued value! For such cases,
this class produces an "empty" value that will really overwrite the
previous one.

whose attributes are determined by parameter "fields" that, similarly to
the List field, determines sub-data for every entry in the dict. This
field is build on top of the List field.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.group.UiGroup.__init__
documentation:
A UiGroup can group various kinds of elements: fields, searches,
transitions..., The type of content that one may find in this group
is given in p_content.
* p_group      is the Group instance corresponding to this UiGroup;
* p_page       is the Page instance where the group is rendered (for
               transitions, it corresponds to a virtual page
               "workflow");
* p_className  is the name of the class that holds the elements to
               group.

any other p_klass field, via expression

                    instance.[field name]

this method, called at server startup, injects all switch sub-fields
as p_klass attributes.

Complex field made of several sub-sets of fields among which only one is
chosen. This field allows to have a part of a form being variable.
The selected sub-set depends on a master/slave relationship that must be
established between a Switch field and some other master field.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.computed.Computed.isCompleteValue
documentation: If you build a custom widget with a Computed field, Appy can't tell if the
value in your widget is complete or not. So it returns True by default.
It is up to you, in method obj.validate, to perform a complete validation,
including verifying if there is a value if your field is required.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.computed.Computed.freeze
documentation:
Normally, no field value is stored for a Computed field: the value is
computed on-the-fly by self.method. But if you freeze it, a value is
stored: either p_value if not None, or the result of calling
self.method else. Once a Computed field value has been frozen,
everytime its value will be requested, the frozen value will be
returned and self.method will not be called anymore. Note that the
frozen value can be unfrozen (see method below).

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.computed.Computed.unfreeze
documentation: Removes the database value that was frozen for this field on p_obj

classes.name: py2.appy.fields.search.Initiator
documentation:
When a query is rendered in a popup, the "initiator", in the main page,
can be:
* (a) some object, in view or edit mode, displaying a given Ref field
      for which the popup is used to select one or more objects to link;
* (b) some class for which we must create an instance from a template;
      the popup is used to select such a template object.

This class is the abstract class for 2 concrete initiator classes:
RefInitiator (for case a) and TemplateInitiator (for case b).
Initiators for searches whose results are shown in popups --------------------

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.RefInitiator.showCheckboxes
documentation:
We must show object checkboxes if self.field is multivalued: indeed,
in this case, several objects can be selected in the popup.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.RefInitiator.jsSelectOne
documentation:
Generates the Javascript code to execute when a single object is
selected in the popup.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.RefInitiator.jsSelectMany
documentation:
Generates the Javascript code to execute when several objects are
selected in the popup.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.RefInitiator.getAjaxParams
documentation:
Get initiator-specific parameters for retriggering the Ajax
request for refreshing objects in the popup.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.TemplateInitiator.showCheckboxes
documentation:
We must hide object checkboxes: only one template object can be
selected.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.TemplateInitiator.jsSelectOne
documentation:
Generates the Javascript code to execute when a single object is
selected in the popup.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.UiSearch.getRootHookId

If there is an initiator, return the hook as defined by it. Else,
return the name of the search.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.UiSearch.showCheckboxes
documentation: When must checkboxes be shown ?

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.UiSearch.getCbJsInit
documentation:
Returns the code that creates JS data structures for storing the
status of checkboxes for every result of this search.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.UiSearch.getModes
documentation:
Gets all the modes applicable when displaying query results (being
instances of p_klass) via this search (p_self). r_ is a list of names
and not a list of Mode instances.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.UiSearch.getTitleMode
py2.appy.fields.search.UiSearch.getResultsTop
documentation:
If p_klass defines something to display on the results page just
before displaying search results, returns it.

Instances of this class are generated on-the-fly for manipulating a
Search instance from the User Interface.

Unmarshalls, from request key "criteria", a dict that was marshalled
from a dict similar to the one stored in attribute "criteria" in
Criteria instances.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.Criteria.getDefault
documentation:
Get the default search criteria that may be defined on the
corresponding Appy class, in field Class.searchAdvanced (field
values, sort filters, etc), and return it if found.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.search.Criteria.getFromRequest

Retrieve search criteria from the request after the user has filled
an advanced search form and perform some transforms on it to produce

Represents a set of search criteria from a custom search

classes.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Role
documentation: Represents a role, be it local or global

classes.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.getName
documentation: Returns the name for this state in workflow p_wf

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.copyPerms
documentation: Gets a deep copy of this state's permissions dict

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.getRole
documentation:
p_role can be the name of a role or a Role instance. If it is the
name of a role, this method returns self.usedRoles[role] if it
exists, or creates a Role instance, puts it in self.usedRoles and
returns it else. If it is a Role instance, the method stores it in
self.usedRoles if it is not in it yet and returns it.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.standardizeRoles
documentation:
This method converts, within self.permissions, every role to a
Role instance. Every used role is stored in self.usedRoles.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.addRoles
documentation:
Adds p_roles in self.permissions. p_roles can be a role name, a Role
instance or a list of names and/or Role instances. If p_permissions
is specified, roles are added to those permissions only. Else, roles
are added for every permission within self.permissions.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.removeRoles
documentation:
Removes p_roleNames within dict self.permissions. If p_permissions is
specified, removal is restricted to those permissions. Else, removal
occurs throughout the whole dict self.permissions.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.setRoles
documentation:
Sets p_rolesNames for p_permissions if not empty, for every
permission in self.permissions else.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.replaceRole
documentation:
Replaces p_oldRoleName by p_newRoleName. If p_permissions is
specified, the replacement is restricted to those permissions. Else,
replacements apply to the whole dict self.permissions.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.copyRoles
documentation:
Overrides p_destPermission's roles with (a deep copy of)
p_sourcePermission's roles.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.State.isIsolated
documentation:
Returns True if, from this state, we cannot reach another state. The
workflow class is given in p_wf. Modifying a workflow for getting a
state with auto-transitions only is a common technique for disabling
a state in a workflow.
Represents a workflow state

classes.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.standardiseStates
documentation:
Get p_states as a list or a list of lists. Indeed, the user may also
specify p_states as a tuple or tuple of tuples. Having lists allows
us to easily perform changes in states if required.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.getName
documentation: Returns the name for this state in workflow p_wf

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.getEndStateName
documentation:
Returns the name of p_self's end state. If p_self is a
multi-transition, the name of a specific p_startStateName can be
given.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.getUsedRoles
documentation: self.condition can specify a role

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.isSingle
documentation:
If this transition is only defined between 2 states, returns True.
Else, returns False.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.addAction
documentation: Adds an p_action in self.action

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition._replaceStateIn
documentation: Replace p_oldState by p_newState in p_states.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.replaceState
documentation: Replace p_oldState by p_newState in self.states.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.removeState
documentation:
For a multi-state transition, this method removes every state pair
containing p_state.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.setState
documentation:
Configure this transition as being an auto-transition on p_state.
This can be useful if, when changing a workflow, one wants to remove
a state by isolating him from the rest of the state diagram and
disable some transitions by making them auto-transitions of this
disabled state.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.isShowable
documentation: Is this transition showable?

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.hasState
documentation:
If p_isFrom is True, this method returns True if p_state is a
starting state for p_self. If p_isFrom is False, this method returns
True if p_state is an ending state for p_self.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.replaceRoleInCondition
documentation:
When self.condition is a tuple or list, this method replaces role
p_old by p_new. p_old and p_new can be strings or Role instances.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.isTriggerable
documentation: Can this transition be triggered on p_obj ?

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.executeAction
documentation: Executes the action related to this transition

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.executeCommonAction
documentation:
Executes the action that is common to any transition, named
"onTrigger" on the workflow class by convention. The common action is
executed before the transition-specific action (if any).

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.trigger
documentation:
This method triggers this transition (named p_name) on p_obj. If
p_doAction is False, the action that must normally be executed after
the transition has been triggered will not be executed. If
p_doHistory is False, there will be no trace from this transition
triggering in p_obj's history. If p_doSay is False, we consider
the transition as being triggered programmatically, and no message is
returned to the user. If p_reindex is False, object reindexing will
be performed by the caller method. If p_data is specified, it is a
dict containing custom data that will be integrated into the history
event. Be careful: if keys in this dict correspond to standard event
keys ("action", "time"...) they will override it.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.onUiRequest
documentation: Executed when a user wants to trigger this transition from the UI

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Transition.getBack
documentation:
Returns the name of the transition (in p_workflow) that "cancels" the
triggering of p_transition and allows to go back to p_transition's
start state.
Represents a workflow transition

classes.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.UiTransition

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.UiTransition.getBackHook
documentation:
If, when the transition has been triggered, we must ajax-refresh some
part of the page, this method will return the ID of the corresponding
DOM node. Else (ie, the entire page needs to be refreshed), it
returns None.
Represents a widget that displays a transition

classes.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Permission

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.Permission.getName
documentation: Returns the name of this permission

If you need to define a specific read or write permission for some field
on a gen-class, you use the specific boolean attrs
"specificReadPermission" or "specificWritePermission". When you want to
refer to those specific read or write permissions when
defining a workflow, for example, you need to use instances of
"ReadPermission" and "WritePermission", the 2 children classes of this
class. For example, if you need to refer to write permission of
attribute "t1" of class A, write: WritePermission("A.t1") or
WritePermission("x.y.A.t1") if class A is not in the same module as
where you instantiate the class.

Note that this holds only if you use attributes "specificReadPermission"
and "specificWritePermission" as booleans. When defining named
(string) permissions, for referring to it you simply use those strings,
you do not create instances of ReadPermission or WritePermission.

classes.name: py2.appy.fields.workflow.WorkflowAuthenticated

documentation: Computes the number of hours between h1 and h2
Class methods

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.hour.Hour.getMinutes
documentation:
Converts an Hour p_value ~(i_hours, i_minutes)~ into an integer
number of minutes.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.hour.Hour.fromMinutes
documentation:
Converts a number of p_minutes into a Hour-compliant tuple
(i_hour, i_minutes)

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.hour.Hour.fromString
documentation:
Converts the string representation p_s of an hour (ie, "1:15",
"-0:30", "165:34", "00:00"...) into a integer number of minutes.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.hour.Hour.getDuration
documentation:
Returns the duration, in minutes, of the interval [start, end],
"start" and "end" each being of the form ~(i_hours, i_minutes)~.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.hour.Hour.formatDuration
documentation: Returns a formatted version of this number of p_minutes

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.hour.Hour.getRanges
documentation:
Return a tuple of tuples representing the ranges of hours included
in time interval (start, end). p_start and p_end are each a tuple of
the form ~(i_hour, i_minutes)~.

methods.name: py2.appy.fields.hour.Hour.intersection
documentation:
Compute and return the number of minutes being common to p_rangeA and
p_rangeB. Each range is of the form

    ((i_startHour, i_startMinutes), (i_endHour, i_endMinutes))

Never call this method with p_recurse=False.
Field allowing to define an hour independently of a complete date
